paragraphs.text: 2. What region is pain present in?
3. Do you have a recent history of fall or trauma in the affected region?
5. What is the type of your pain?
7. Your pain is relieved by rest?
8. Do you have swelling after any activity?
9. Is there tenderness at patellar facets?
10. Is there crepitation with knee movement?
12. Does the pain radiate down the leg?
13. Is there any previous history of knee injury or instability?
a. Yes (Rule out prior ligament/meniscus injury)
14. Do you experience pain when descending stairs more than ascending stairs?
Yes (Strong PFPS indicator due to high patellofemoral joint compression)
15. Do you experience pain when kneeling?
17. Do you experience pain when sitting for prolonged periods with the knee bent (Cinema sign)?
18. Do you experience pain when standing up after prolonged sitting?
19. Is the pain poorly localized or difficult to pinpoint with one finger?
a. Yes (Diffuse anterior knee pain typical of PFPS)
b. No (Localized pain suggests tendinopathy, bursitis, or meniscus pathology)
20. Did the symptoms begin after increased activity, overuse, or training intensity?
a. Yes (Common overload mechanism in PFPS)
21. Do you experience locking or catching of the knee?
a. Yes (Rule out meniscus tear or loose body)
22. Do you feel instability or giving way of the knee?
a. Yes (Rule out ligament injury or patellar instability)
23. Do you feel like your kneecap moves out of place or dislocates?
a. Yes (Rule out patellar instability or subluxation)
24. Is the pain localized specifically to the inferior pole of the patella?
25. Do you experience hip pain along with knee pain?
a. Yes (Rule out referred pain from hip pathology such as FAI or slipped epiphysis)
26. Do symptoms worsen with prolonged knee flexion such as driving, studying, or sitting in meetings?
27. Do symptoms improve with activity modification or reduced loading?
1. Is the Patellar alignment abnormal (lateral tracking)?
a. Yes – not required for PFPS
b. No (Normal is typical for PFPS; atrophy suggests chronicity or other pathology)
a. Patient is seated with knee slightly flexed. Examiner applies resistance as the patient extends the knee.
b. Sign is positive if pain is felt over the anterior patella.
If positive, indicates patellofemoral joint irritation associated with PFPS.
a. Patient stands on a 20 cm step and slowly lowers the contralateral heel to the ground while examiner observes knee alignment.
b. Sign is positive if there is pain over the anterior knee or if the knee collapses medially (dynamic valgus).
If positive, suggests PFPS and poor lower limb control.
a. Patient is supine with knee extended. Examiner applies gentle downward pressure on the superior pole (base) of the patella while patient contracts the quadriceps.
b. Sign is positive if anterior knee pain is reproduced.
If positive, indicates patellofemoral joint dysfunction and PFPS.
a. Patient is supine and relaxed. Examiner pushes the patella laterally.
b. Sign is positive if patient shows apprehension or contracts the quadriceps.
If positive, suggests lateral patellar instability, which may co-exist with PFPS.
a. Patient is supine with knee extended. Examiner lifts the lateral edge of the patella.
b. Sign is positive if lateral patellar edge cannot reach horizontal.
If positive, suggests tight lateral structures contributing to PFPS.
a. Patient is seated and actively flexes and extends the knee while examiner palpates the patellofemoral joint.
b. Sign is positive if grinding, crepitus, or anterior knee pain is felt.
If positive, indicates PFPS or patellofemoral joint irritation.
a. Patient is supine with knee extended. Examiner glides the patella medially and laterally.
b. Sign is positive if there is pain or abnormal patellar mobility.
If positive, suggests patellar maltracking and PFPS.
2. Do you have recent twisting injury?
3. What region is pain present in?
5. Do you experience locking or catching around the knee?
6. Do you hear audible sounds during activity
9. Do you experience swelling within 24 hours?
10. Is there instability or giving way at the knee?
11. Do you have previous knee injury or surgery?
12. What is the duration of your pain
3. Is there Range of motion limitation?
4. Is there catching / clicking on ROM
a. Patient is supine. Examiner flexes the hip and knee fully, then rotates the tibia internally and externally while extending the knee.
b. Sign is positive if there is a palpable click, snap, or pain along the joint line.
If positive, suggests a medial or lateral meniscus tear, depending on rotation (internal = lateral meniscus, external = medial meniscus).
a. Patient lies prone with knee flexed to 90°. Examiner applies downward pressure along the tibia while rotating it internally and externally.
b. Sign is positive if pain is elicited along the joint line during compression.
a. Patient stands on one leg with knee slightly flexed (~20°). Patient rotates the body and knee internally and externally three times.
b. Sign is positive if pain or a sense of locking/catching occurs along the joint line.
If positive, suggests a meniscal tear.
a. Examiner palpates along the medial and lateral joint lines of the knee while patient is relaxed.
b. Sign is positive if localized tenderness is felt.
If positive, indicates possible meniscal pathology.
a. Patient is supine. Examiner fully flexes the knee and then allows it to passively extend.
b. Sign is positive if the knee does not fully extend (blocked) or produces a springy end-feel.
If positive, suggests a displaced meniscal fragment (e.g., bucket-handle tear).
a. Patient stands with knees flexed and externally rotates both feet to test the medial meniscus; internally rotates both feet to test the lateral meniscus.
b. Sign is positive if pain or clicking occurs along the joint line.
3. What is the nature of the pain you experience?
4. Is your pain aggravated by stretching/contraction?
5. Is your pain relieved by rest?
6. Does tenderness at affect the muscle?
1. Is there palpable muscle gap or spasm
a. How to perform: Patient contracts the affected muscle against resistance at mid-range.
b. Positive: Pain at the site
a. How to perform: Examiner stretches the affected muscle slowly.
b. Positive: Pain at the site
2. What is the Mechanism of your injury?
4. Is there audible pop at time of injury?
5. What region is your pain present in?
7. Is there any Instability / giving way?
5. Is there tenderness at ligament insertion
a. With pt supine, knee flexed to 30°, examiner stabilizes the femur and applies a valgus force to the knee.
b. Positive sign = Pain or gapping at the medial knee joint line indicates MCL injury.
a. Grade I: Pain without laxity (<5 mm)
b. Grade II: Pain + moderate laxity (5–10 mm)
c. Grade III: Pain + marked laxity (>10 mm)
a. With pt supine, knee flexed to 30°, examiner stabilizes the femur and applies a varus force to the knee.
b. Positive sign = Pain or gapping at the lateral knee joint line indicates LCL injury.
a. Grade I: Pain without laxity (<5 mm)
b. Grade II: Pain + moderate laxity (5–10 mm)
c. Grade III: Pain + marked laxity (>10 mm)
a. Knee flexed ~30°, examiner stabilizes femur and pulls tibia anteriorly at the knee.
b. Positive sign = Soft endpoint or increased anterior tibial translation indicates ACL tear.
a. Knee flexed 90°, examiner pulls tibia anteriorly at the knee.
b. Positive sign = Increased anterior translation indicates ACL injury.
a. Knee flexed 90°, examiner pushes tibia posteriorly at the knee.
b. Positive sign = Posterior translation indicates PCL tear.
a. Hip and knee flexed 90°, examiner observes tibial plateau at the knee.
b. Positive sign = Sagging tibia compared to contralateral knee indicates PCL injury.
a. Knee flexed 30° and 90°, examiner externally rotates tibia at the knee bilaterally.
b. Positive sign = ≥10° external rotation difference indicates PLC injury.
a. Examiner slowly extends knee while applying valgus + external rotation forces at the knee.
b. Positive sign = Clunk at ~30° flexion indicates PLC injury.
2. Is your pain aggravated by activity?
4. Is your pain relieved by rest?
5. Do you hear crepitus sound during movement?
7. Is there swelling or bony enlargement?
8. Is there any instability / giving way?
9. Is your pain prolonged with sitting or after rest?
10. Do you have difficulty climbing stairs or squatting?
11. Is there any previous knee injury or surgery?
4. Is there limited range of motion (flexion/extension)?
5. Is there bony enlargement / osteophytes palpated?
a. Patient supine with knee extended. Examiner applies downward pressure on the patella while asking the patient to contract the quadriceps.
b. Positive sign = Pain or crepitus at the patellofemoral joint, suggesting patellofemoral OA.
a. Examiner lifts the lateral edge of the patella while stabilizing the medial edge.
b. Positive sign = Limited tilt or pain indicates tight lateral structures or OA changes.
a. Examiner moves the knee through full flexion and extension, palpating and listening for audible or palpable crepitus.
b. Positive sign = Crepitus or grinding indicates articular cartilage degeneration.
a. Examiner passively flexes and extends the knee while noting pain, stiffness, or limited ROM.
b. Positive sign = Reduced flexion or extension suggests joint degeneration.
a. Examiner palpates medial and lateral tibiofemoral joint lines.
b. Positive sign = Pain at joint line indicates tibiofemoral OA involvement.
a. Ask patient to perform activities such as squatting, stair climbing, or prolonged sitting.
b. Positive sign = Pain reproduction during these activities supports OA diagnosis.
2. Do you recently have twisting injury or direct blow to your knee?
3. Did you feel the kneecap move out of place?
4. Did it reduce (go back in) on its own?
5. Do you have swelling within 2 hours of injury?
b. Medial joint line (Rule out meniscus)
7. Is there instability or repeated episodes?
8. Is your sleep affected due to knee pain?
9. Does your pain radiate down to the leg?
11. Is your pain aggravated by activity / knee motion?
12. Do you experience locking of the knee after trauma?
2. Is there apprehension when knee extended?
8. Is there patella tracking abnormality on flexion/extension?
a. Patient lies supine with the knee extended. Examiner gently pushes the patella laterally.
b. Positive sign = Patient shows apprehension or contracts quadriceps to prevent lateral displacement, indicating patellar instability.
a. Observe the patella during active knee flexion and extension.
b. Positive sign = Patella deviates laterally or fails to track smoothly within the trochlear groove, suggesting abnormal motion or previous dislocation.
a. Patient lies supine, knee extended. Examiner gently displaces patella medially and laterally.
b. Positive sign = Patella moves excessively beyond normal range (>50% of patella width), indicating generalized patellar laxity.
a. Measure the angle between the ASIS–patella line and patella–tibial tuberosity line with knee extended.
b. Positive sign = Q-angle increased (>15° in men, >20° in women), suggesting malalignment predisposing to lateral patellar dislocation.
Observation for Patella Alta / Genu Recurvatum
a. Inspect patella position in full extension and assess knee hyperextension.
b. Positive sign = High-riding patella (patella alta) or excessive hyperextension, indicating structural risk factors for dislocation.
a. Lateral and axial X-rays; MRI if soft tissue assessment needed.
b. Positive sign = Subluxation/dislocation, osteochondral fractures, or medial patellofemoral ligament tear.
1. Do you have recent fall, RTA, or high-impact trauma?
a. Yes (Suggests tibial plateau fracture / patella fracture)
2. Is there direct blow to front of knee?
3. Is there immediate inability to bear weight?
a. Yes (Suggests tibial plateau fracture or severe patella fracture)
a. Severe sharp pain (Suggests acute fracture)
6. What is the onset of pain
7. Do you have difficulty straightening the knee?
a. Yes (Suggests patella fracture / extensor mechanism injury)
8. Unable to perform straight leg raise?
a. Yes (Strong indicator of patella fracture)
10. Do you have feeling of knee instability after trauma?
a. Yes (Suggests tibial plateau fracture with ligament involvement)
11. Any history of snapping or cracking sound at injury?
2. Is the tenderness directly over your patella?
3. Is the tenderness over upper tibia (tibial plateau)?
5. Is there bruising around knee or upper leg?
6. Do you experience severely restricted knee ROM due to pain?
7. Do you feel gap over patella on palpation?
a. Examiner observes the anterior knee in resting position.
b. Positive sign = Visible swelling, bruising, or deformity, suggesting patella fracture.
a. Patient lies supine with both legs extended. Examiner asks the patient to lift the affected leg straight up without bending the knee.
b. Positive sign = Inability to lift the leg actively, indicating disruption of the extensor mechanism due to patella fracture.
a. Patient lies supine with knee relaxed. Examiner palpates the patella gently using fingers to feel for tenderness, irregularity, or gap.
b. Positive sign = Localized severe tenderness, palpable gap, or irregularity, indicating patella fracture.
a. Patient sits or lies supine. Examiner asks patient to actively straighten the knee from a flexed position.
b. Positive sign = Inability or severe pain during active extension, indicating patella fracture or extensor mechanism disruption.
4. Patellar Compression Test (Modified for fracture screening)
a. Patient lies supine with knee extended. Examiner gently presses patella posteriorly toward femur.
b. Positive sign = Severe localized pain, suggesting patella fracture (avoid excessive force).
a. Radiographic imaging of the knee joint.
b. Positive sign = Visible fracture line, displacement, or comminution of patella.
SPECIAL CONFIRMATORY TESTS – TIBIAL PLATEAU FRACTURE
a. Patient lies supine. Examiner applies gentle axial compression through the heel toward the tibia.
b. Positive sign = Severe pain at tibial plateau region, indicating possible fracture.
a. Patient lies supine or sits comfortably. Examiner palpates medial and lateral tibial plateau.
b. Positive sign = Localized severe tenderness, indicating tibial plateau fracture.
a. Patient attempts to stand or bear weight on affected limb.
b. Positive sign = Inability or severe pain during weight-bearing, indicating tibial plateau fracture.
4. Varus and Valgus Stress Test (performed cautiously)
a. Patient lies supine. Examiner applies gentle inward (varus) and outward (valgus) forces at knee.
b. Positive sign = Severe pain or instability, suggesting tibial plateau fracture or associated ligament injury.
a. Radiological imaging performed. CT scan provides best fracture detail; MRI assesses soft tissue injury.
b. Positive sign = Visible fracture line, depression, or displacement of tibial plateau.
2. Does your activity involve repetitive knee bending (running, cycling)?
a. Lateral (outer) side of knee (Classic ITBS location)
5. Is the pain aggravated by running?
a. Yes, especially long-distance running (Classic ITBS symptom)
6. Is the pain worse when running downhill or descending stairs?
7. Is the pain relieved by rest?
8. Does the pain occur after specific distance/time of running?
9. Is there burning or sharp pain on outer knee?
10. Does the pain radiate up thigh or down lateral leg?
11. Is there snapping sensation on outer knee?
12. Is there history of recent increase in training intensity or distance?
13. Is the pain absent at rest but appears during activity?
1. Is there tenderness over the lateral femoral epicondyle?
a. Yes (Most important objective sign of ITBS)
2. Is the pain reproduced at 30° knee flexion?
3. Is there swelling over the lateral knee?
a. Yes (May be present in ITBS)
4. Is there pain during repeated knee flexion and extension?
5. Is there tight iliotibial band on palpation?
a. Yes (Major biomechanical cause of ITBS)
7. Is there pain during single-leg squat?
a. Yes (Helps rule out ligament injury, supports ITBS diagnosis)
SPECIAL CONFIRMATORY TESTS – ILIOTIBIAL BAND SYNDROME
a. Patient lies supine. Examiner flexes knee to 90° and applies pressure over lateral femoral epicondyle while slowly extending the knee.
b. Positive sign = Pain at approximately 30° knee flexion, indicating ITBS.
a. Patient lies on unaffected side. Examiner extends and abducts the hip, then slowly lowers the leg.
b. Positive sign = Leg remains elevated and does not drop, indicating tight iliotibial band.
a. Patient stands and performs squat while examiner applies pressure over lateral femoral epicondyle.
b. Positive sign = Pain at approximately 30° knee flexion, indicating ITBS.
a. Patient lies supine at edge of table. Examiner observes hip and thigh position.
b. Positive sign = Tight lateral thigh indicating IT band tightness.
5. Palpation Test of Lateral Femoral Epicondyle
a. Examiner palpates lateral femoral epicondyle with knee flexed and extended.
b. Positive sign = Localized tenderness, indicating ITBS.
a. Used if diagnosis unclear or to rule out other pathology.
b. Positive sign = IT band thickening or inflammation.
a. 15–40 years (Common in jumping athletes – jumper’s knee)
b. >40 years (More common in quadriceps tendinopathy)
2. Does your activity involves jumping, sprinting, or frequent knee loading?
a. Inferior pole of patella (Classic patellar tendinopathy)
b. Superior pole of patella (Suggests quadriceps tendinopathy)
5. Is there pain during loading activities (jumping, squatting, running)?
6. Does the pain increases with higher load (deep squat, higher jump)?
a. Yes (Dose-dependent pain – key diagnostic feature)
7. Is the pain relieved immediately after stopping activity?
8. Do you experience pain at rest
a. No pain at rest (Typical early tendinopathy)
b. Pain at rest (suggests severe or other pathology)
9. Do you experience pain during decline walking or descending stairs?
10. Do you experience pain during prolonged sitting?
11 Is there history of repetitive sports (basketball, volleyball, running)?
12. Does the pain improves after warm-up activity?
1. Is there pain during resisted knee extension?
3. Does the pain increases with deeper squat?
5. Is there pain during hopping test?
6. Is there reduced flexibility of quadriceps or hamstrings?
7. Is there normal passive knee ROM?
a. Yes (Helps differentiate from joint pathology)
SPECIAL CONFIRMATORY TESTS – PATELLAR / QUADRICEPS TENDINOPATHY
Patient stands on 25° decline board and performs squat.
Positive sign = Localized pain at inferior pole of patella, indicating patellar tendinopathy.
Patient sits with knee flexed. Examiner resists knee extension.
Positive sign = Localized tendon pain, indicating patellar or quadriceps tendinopathy.
Patient performs single-leg squat on decline surface.
Positive sign = Reproduction of localized tendon pain.
Examiner palpates inferior and superior poles of patella.
Positive sign = Localized tenderness at tendon insertion.
Patient performs repeated hopping on affected leg.
Positive sign = Pain localized to patellar or quadriceps tendon.
a. Used to confirm tendon pathology if needed.
b. Positive sign = Tendon thickening, degeneration, or structural abnormality.
40–70 years (Common in middle-aged and older adults)
Female (Higher prevalence, especially with knee valgus and OA)
Medial knee, 5–7 cm below joint line (Classic location of pes anserinus bursitis)
Is the pain aggravated by stair climbing?
Is the pain aggravated by sit-to-stand movement?
Is the pain aggravated by running or walking?
Is the pain aggravated by pivoting or side-to-side movement?
Is the pain relieved by rest?
Is there any history of repetitive knee bending activity?
Is there any history of knee osteoarthritis?
Is there any history of obesity?
Is there any history of flat feet?
Is there any history of hamstring tightness?
Is there any history of direct trauma to medial knee?
Is the pain present at night when lying on affected side?
Is there localized tenderness 5–7 cm below medial joint line?
Is there swelling over pes anserinus region?
Is the pain present on palpation of pes anserinus region?
Do you experience pain with resisted knee flexion?
Do you experience pain with resisted hip adduction?
Do you experience pain during knee extension?
Is medial joint line tenderness absent?
SPECIAL CONFIRMATORY TESTS – PES ANSERINUS BURSITIS
Patient lies supine with knee extended. Examiner palpates 5–7 cm below medial joint line.
Positive sign = Localized tenderness, indicating pes anserinus bursitis.
Patient flexes knee while examiner applies resistance.
Positive sign = Pain at pes anserinus region, indicating bursitis.
Patient performs hip adduction while examiner applies resistance.
Positive sign = Pain at pes anserinus insertion.
Positive sign = Pain at pes anserinus region, indicating involvement.
Differentiation Test from Medial Meniscus Injury
Examiner palpates medial joint line and pes anserinus separately.
Positive sign = Pain localized below joint line (pes anserinus), not at joint line.
Deep knee pain, poorly localized (Typical OCD presentation)
Is there history of repetitive sports activity (running, jumping, football)?
Yes (Major risk factor due to repetitive microtrauma)
Is there history of knee trauma?
Is the pain aggravated by activity?
Is the pain relieved by rest?
Is there knee swelling after activity?
Do you experience knee catching sensation?
Do you experience knee giving way?
Do you experience stiffness in the knee?
Do symptoms persist for months or years?
Is there localized tenderness over femoral condyle?
Is there reduced knee range of motion?
Is there pain during passive knee extension?
Is locking during knee movement observed?
Yes (Compensation due to pain or instability)
Patient lies supine. Examiner flexes knee to 90°. Patient actively extends knee while examiner internally rotates tibia.
Positive sign = Pain between 30°–40° knee extension that is relieved with external rotation of tibia, indicating OCD of medial femoral condyle.
Patient knee flexed to 90°. Examiner palpates femoral condyles.
Positive sign = Localized pain over femoral condyle, indicating OCD lesion.
Examiner strokes medial knee upward and lateral knee downward.
Positive sign = Fluid movement or bulge, indicating joint effusion associated with OCD.
Positive sign = Deep knee pain, indicating articular surface pathology.
Posterior knee (popliteal region) (Classic Baker’s cyst location)
Do you notice swelling behind the knee?
Do you feel a lump or mass behind the knee?
Is the pain aggravated by activity (walking, running, standing)?
Do you feel tightness behind the knee?
Do you experience stiffness behind the knee?
Do you experience pain during full knee extension?
Do you experience difficulty bending your knee fully?
Do symptoms worsen with prolonged standing?
Do you experience calf pain without injury?
Is there history of knee osteoarthritis or inflammatory arthritis?
Is there history of meniscus injury?
Is there visible swelling in popliteal region?
Is there palpable mass in popliteal fossa?
Is the mass more prominent with knee extension?
Does the mass reduce or disappears with knee flexion?
Is there tenderness in the popliteal region?
Patient lies supine. Examiner palpates popliteal mass while knee is extended and then flexed.
Positive sign = Mass becomes firm in extension and softens or disappears in flexion, confirming Baker’s cyst.
Patient prone or supine. Examiner palpates popliteal fossa.
Positive sign = Palpable fluid-filled mass, indicating cyst.
Positive sign = Transillumination suggests fluid-filled cyst.
Examiner performs stroke test or bulge test.
Positive sign = Fluid accumulation present, indicating intra-articular pathology associated with cyst.
Do you have the ability to bear weight?
Unable to bear weight (Strong indicator of septic arthritis)
Is there any history of recent infection (skin, urinary, respiratory)?
Is there any history of fever?
Is there any history of joint swelling?
Is there any history of recent joint surgery, injection, or prosthesis?
Is there any history of diabetes, HIV, or immunosuppression?
Is there any history of rheumatoid arthritis or osteoarthritis?
Is there any history of recent trauma or open wound near joint?
Is there severe pain during passive joint movement?
Is active and passive ROM severely restricted?
Does the patient appear systemically ill?
Is there tenderness on palpation of entire joint?
Is the patient unable to tolerate minimal joint movement?
Examiner gently moves the patient’s knee through passive flexion and extension.
Positive sign = Severe pain even with minimal passive movement, indicating joint infection.
Examiner compares temperature of affected and unaffected knee.
Positive sign = Affected knee significantly warmer, indicating infection.
Sterile needle inserted into joint space to withdraw synovial fluid.
Examiner strokes medial and lateral knee.
Positive sign = Significant fluid accumulation, suggesting infection.
May show joint swelling (early), joint destruction (late)
Detects early joint infection and soft tissue involvement
⚠ Avoid aggressive testing. Severe pain with passive movement is positive
Thumb, index, middle, and radial half of ring finger (CLASSIC CTS)
Whole hand (Consider proximal nerve or systemic issue)
Do symptoms wake you from sleep?
Do you shake your hand to relieve symptoms?
Does pain radiate above the wrist?
Is there reduced sensation in median nerve distribution?
Patient flexes wrists fully and holds position.
Sign is positive if numbness/tingling occurs.
Sign is positive if tingling in median nerve distribution.
Examiner applies direct pressure over carpal tunnel.
Sign is positive if symptoms are reproduced.
Is there pain with lifting objects (e.g., child, kettle)?
Is there swelling near thumb base?
Is there ttenderness over radial styloid?
Is there pain with resisted thumb extension/abduction?
Yes (Abductor Pollicis Longus (APL) and Extensor Pollicis Brevis (EPB) involvement)
Patient makes a fist with thumb inside fingers. Examiner ulnar deviates wrist.
Sign is positive if sharp pain at radial wrist.
WHAT Test (Wrist Hyperflexion Abduction of Thumb)
Patient hyperflexes wrist and abducts thumb against resistance.
Sign is positive if pain is reproduced.
Is there any history of trauma?
What is the mechanism of injury
Is the pain aggravated by movement?
Does the patient experience pain with ligament stress?
Is there severe pain with deformity?
Is the patient unable to move the wrist?
a. Examiner applies stress to specific ligaments.
b. Sign is positive if pain/laxity occurs.
Is there any history of trauma?
Do you experience pain when clicking or catching?
Is there tenderness over ulnar wrist?
Wrist in ulnar deviation + axial load + rotation
Patient pushes up from chair using hands
6.	Does the size of the lump change?
8.	Is there any history of trauma?
Is there visible or palpable cystic swelling?
a. Light is applied through the swelling
b. Positive if light passes through
Dorsal radial forearm (4–6 cm proximal to wrist) (KEY SIGN)
Do you feel creaking or squeaking?
Is there tenderness proximal to radial styloid?
Is there crepitus with wrist movement?
Is there pain with resisted wrist extension?
a. Patient extends wrist against resistance
Is there any history of trauma?
Did you ignore the injury initially?
Is there tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox?
Is there pain with axial thumb compression?
Do you experience persistent pain after fall despite normal X-ray?
How will you describe the pain?
Do you experience pain at rest or night?
Is there any history of repetitive trauma or loading?
Is there tenderness over lunate (central dorsal wrist)?
Do you experience progressive stiffness and pain?
Do you experience night pain not relieved by rest?
b. Positive if central wrist pain
If positive, suggests intra-articular pathology (Kienböck’s possible)
